topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The laptop screen glowed with the relentless, pristine text. I found it tucked beneath a stack of unrelated grocery lists—a manuscript. My breath hitched, a sharp, involuntary intake of air that tasted like copper. It was *my* life, meticulously chronicled, every embarrassing glance, every quiet triumph, every poorly timed sigh. A cold, hard knot tightened in my chest, squeezing the air from my lungs. I didn't move for a full minute, just staring at the words, the sheer audacity of it making my jaw clench so hard I thought my teeth might crack. When he finally walked in, humming off-key, I didn't look up. "What are you doing?" I managed, my voice dangerously flat. He stopped, a slight confusion clouding his features. The silence stretched, thick and suffocating, punctuated only by the frantic drumming of my pulse against my ribs.

[story 2]

I slammed the laptop shut. The sound echoed in the otherwise quiet living room, a violent punctuation mark to a mounting internal storm. My hands were shaking, the tremor running up my arms, making the cheap ceramic mug in my grip feel impossibly heavy. Every line of text, every carefully constructed narrative of my existence, felt like a personal assault. I paced the small space, my footsteps sharp and uneven on the hardwood floor. "Don't you dare," I hissed, the words coming out tight and brittle. When he finally approached, trying to gauge the sudden shift in my demeanor, I turned, my eyes blazing with an intensity I rarely allowed myself to unleash. I didn't raise my voice; instead, I leaned in close, my gaze locking onto his, conveying a deep, unsettling displeasure that demanded an immediate explanation.

[story 3]

It was the quietest discovery, which somehow made the ensuing reaction far more volatile. I found the journal hidden inside a seldom-used book, the handwriting unmistakably his—elegant, flowing, and undeniably focused on my every idiosyncrasy. A wave of pure, unadulterated irritation washed over me, hot and immediate. I didn't scream; screaming felt too messy, too uncontrolled. Instead, I walked directly to him, my movements stiff, deliberate. I held the book out, my fingers trembling slightly as I presented the evidence. "This," I said, my tone dangerously level, every syllable weighted with accusation, "is what you've been doing? Writing me up?" I watched his face shift—the sudden realization dawning, followed by a flicker of something that looked suspiciously like defensiveness. The tension in the room became palpable, a coiled spring ready to snap.
